comments.title: Egregors
comments.content:
Do nations, large corporations, and other "imagined communities" have egregors (or similar entities)?
-Berserker

replies.title: Re: Egregors
replies.content: Very much so. Any time a group of people imagine themselves as sharing a common identity, and have a common emotional relation toward that identity, that combination of imagination and emotion creates an egregor. Some of those egregors are temporary -- if you attended summer camp when you were a kid, you might have noticed the way that the kids who all went to the same camp at the same time built up a collective identity, which faded out once they went home -- but if the identity is enduring and the emotions are strong, you can get a very robust egregor which will influence the people who belong to it in various ways.

replies.title: Re: Egregors
replies.content: Wait…  My current draft of the Green Wizards’ Benevolent and Protective Association’s origin story starts with a poor Ruinman buying a used book and applying its knowledge.  Is this why the people who read Green Wizardry and come to our get-togethers tend to be marginalized tool owning men with hard skills who hold or have held “dirty” jobs?  Rusty at Tower 440

replies.title: Re: Egregors
replies.content: Very likely that's part of it. Another part is simply that people of the sort you've described are more likely to be interested in the kinds of activities that Green Wizardry involves.

replies.title: Re: Egregors
replies.content:
This question inevitably leads me to wonder about granfalloons--the creation of writer Kurt Vonnegut in his novel _Cat's Cradle_ and defined as follows, "granfalloon is a proud and meaningless collection of human beings...." examples including 'Hoosiers" and the Communist Party. The opposite of an egregore? a failure of an egregore? or just a meaningless concept. I have been in covens that ultimately seemed to be granfaloons rather than Vonnegut's term for meaningful group 'karass'. I suspect that for most people the pumped up 'school spirit' expected of American teens is a granfalloon. As would be the company morale promoted by picnics and team-building exercises.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Egregors
replies.content:
That's a very useful concept! A granfalloon might be characterized in occult terms as a group with an egregor that goes nowhere and does nothing. The risk of turning into a granfalloon is something every group has to deal with, and plenty of groups fall into it -- think of those churches and clubs and social groups that simply go through the motions without ever quite managing to do anything but exist.

I'd characterize 'school spirit' and company team-building exercises differently. Members of a granfalloon, as Vonnegut's definition points out, are proud of their membership -- they've got a real emotional commitment to their egregor. My encounters with 'school spirit' and team-building exercises make me think that there's no actual egregor there, no pride actually being felt, no team actually being built -- just people going through the motions, because those in authority over them require them to do so. That's something that deserves a name of its own, but as I'm no Vonnegut, I can't think of one offhand.

replies.title: Re: Egregors
replies.content: How about a fartygras (a "celebration" composed of mostly hot air)? :D

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content:
Thanks JMG! that's how I thought it was. Most discussions (actually all that I have encountered, hence the question) about egregores are in the context of lodges and other explicitly religious/magical institutions.

Do you agree with Mouni Sadhu that egregores can "reincarnate"?

Oh, and this fall my college writing students will have to research an imagined community. I discourage them from choosing a college community precisely because they are temporary and transient. (If they persist, I insist that the will have to use the archives as well. No takers yet....)

I am so tempted to say to the class "this course is intended train your minds not to inform them"...

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content:
Yes, an egregor can "reincarnate." If an organization with a strong egregor goes out of existence, and people come along later and pick up the symbols and identity of that organization and start using them again, the egregor can pop back into being. Sometimes this is good -- the rituals I put into my book The Celtic Golden Dawn had considerably more kick to them than newly invented rituals usually do, because I apparently got close enough to the egregor of one of the old Golden Dawn/Druid hybrid orders to benefit from its egregor -- but you can also get all the problems of the old egregor, and if those were bad enough, the results can be pretty ghastly.

Please do use Fortune's comment! It might get your students thinking...

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content: The Ku Klux Klan egregore reincarnated at least once, in 1915. I'm not certain whether the third revival of the KKK in the 1950s partakes of the original egregore or not.

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content:
I'd say that college egregors would actually be stronger than, say, high-school ones, if for no other reason than that there's a voluntary component to them - typically, a student *chooses* which college they attend, which isn't the case for high school or before.

Granted, they're still temporary and transient, but think about all the people who still pull for their alma mater's football team even 30 years after graduating (whereas they're only likely to care what their old high-school team is doing if they still live in the area).

comments.title: Do spells need to be refreshed.
comments.content: A few months ago I cast my first ever spell.  It was a successful honey jar spell for increased income for our family's small business.  The spell seemed to last for about three months.  Does one cast the same spell again or refresh the original spell?  Or do something altogether different?  Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Do spells need to be refreshed.
replies.content: It's certainly true that workings don't last indefinitely, though the length of time you get an effect out of any given working will vary significantly due to a wide range of factors. Three months isn't bad at all for a beginner! You can do any of the three things you've mentioned; in your place, I'd probably refresh the spell -- since it worked well, everything connected with it will have a certain momentum that will foster success in the future -- and also look up some other spell that will reinforce the same intention, and do that too.

comments.title: Active working for water
comments.content:
Hi,

I've had some good results using the active working for water from the DMH to help with sleep, using the color blue and the intention "I wake up invigorated." (I once tried directly intending to fall asleep, but this didn't work at all - I suspect since I can only imagine falling asleep, not being asleep, so the intentionality was all wrong. Is that accurate?)

My main question is whether this is the sort of thing that's reasonable to do regularly, or if I should be concerned with overusing the exercise or with elemental imbalances or something. Would balancing this out with color breathing the next morning be useful to counteract any of that? For context, I'm currently working through the gate of Fire in the SoP.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Active working for water
replies.content:
Whether or not that sort of thing will bring imbalance depends very much on the personal element. You'll want to pay attention to any sign of a watery imbalance -- whether that takes an emotional form such as passivity, sluggishness, and loss of interest in things, or a physical form such as chills, excessive perspiration, runny colds, and so on. If so, correct by invoking fire; if not, you may need more water in your energy balance, and the sleep working may be giving you that.

Exactly how to phrase an intention that works for you is a personal thing, and so it's not that the intentionality on the earlier phrasing was wrong in some abstract sense -- it just wasn't right for you.

replies.content: What’s the DMH?  I’m looking for an alternative to Ambien.

replies.title: Re: Active working for water
replies.content: My book The Druid Magic Handbook.

comments.title: What Would a Sabian Do?
comments.content:
As you suggested on 7 July, I’ve obtained a copy of Llewellyn George’s A to Z Horoscope Maker and Delineator, and have spent many hours writing out the influences of all the placements and aspects in my horoscope, creating a hand-written document of perhaps 1500 words.

As you’ll recall, I’m focusing on improving my financial situation with a view to moving, so I created a separate document for that topic. I’ve tried to condense and organize it as much as possible to help me think clearly about the matter.

Then I went back to your post to review your instructions for making a synthesis. To my great annoyance, I discovered that the inter-library loan sent me the wrong book, that you warned against: THE NEW Horoscope Maker, etc. AUGH! All my work wasted. However, since this is at present all I’ve got to go on, I’ll have to go with it. Getting the right edition will take considerable time.

You’ve remarked more than once that fools are ruled by their stars, but the wise rule them. Now that I have something like a (ponderous, possibly dubious) delineation of my chart, how may I best use that information?

There are some disturbing things in it: for example, unpleasant health issues and end-of-life issues. In what ways would a classic sage - say, a Sabian of Harran - use such foreknowledge?

Kevin

replies.title: Re: What Would a Sabian Do?
replies.content:
As you synthesize, break it down into areas of interest -- Personality, Health, Wealth, Career, and so on. In each of these, note down the strengths and weaknesses. Then start exploring ways to capitalize on the strengths and shore up the weaknesses. If your horoscope shows susceptibility to health problems, look into what you can do to keep yourself healthy despite that; if your horoscope offers suggestions for a career, explore options you might not otherwise have thought of, and so on.

The new edition isn't useless, btw -- it's just that the older one is so much better.

replies.title: Re: What Would a Sabian Do?
replies.content:
Thanks! I will proceed as you have suggested. It seems to me pretty much a common sense approach, operating on both the material and not-so-material planes.

I’ll try to obtain the superior edition. If all else fails, I may have to make a trip to the nearest UC library.

Kevin

comments.title: archetypes
comments.content:
I've been mulling over a few of the Kek War essay themes, and I have a few questions regarding archetypes.
1. Are archetypes best understood as having full agency (i.e. they seize the thinker and the vehicle) or as being partially controlled by our own minds (i.e. we project the archetypes onto others)?
2. When we find our thinking shaped by archetypes in less than helpful ways, is there a trick to getting our thinking free of such patterns? I'm thinking particularly here of falling in love and falling in hate...Speaking from a personal situation, simple awareness of the archetype at work has not been enough to break its hold over me. Sorry if you answered this somewhere in the comments--I was unable to locate it.

replies.title: Re: archetypes
replies.content:
1) They have full agency. If anything, we have less agency than they do.

2) You can't get your thinking free of an archetype -- archetypes are the basis of all human thought. By bringing the archetypes into consciousness, we learn to work with them rather than being their playthings. If you fall in love with somebody, the anima or animus archetype's going to be involved no matter what, but if you're conscious of that, you can learn to get some perspective on its influence, and recognize when what you're feeling has nothing to do with the other person and everything to do with the way the archetype has constellated in your mind -- which improves communication no end!

comments.title: strength training and occultism
comments.content:
Hey, JMG!

Last week you mentioned (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/31850.html?thread=1850986#cmt1850986) "the substantial overlap between old-fashioned strength training and occultism", and I was wondering if you would expatiate a bit about that. I'm guessing the overlapping concern is the training of the will?

replies.title: Re: strength training and occultism
replies.content:
Oh, it goes far beyond that! Back in the glory days of physical culture, a lot of people who were into strength training used various occult disciplines alongside their physical exercises. Examples? Top of the list was the amazing Joseph Greenstein, a diminutive Polish-American Jew who could tie a #2 iron horseshoe into an overhand knot with his bare hands. He was a Cabalist, and used Cabalistic prayers and meditations to direct his vital energy into his feats of strength. Another good example is Genevieve Stebbins, one of the first people to focus on teaching exercise to women; she was also a leading adept in the Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor, one of the big occult schools of that time, and included a lot of occultism in her instructions. Nearly everybody knew how to use basic New Thought techniques such as affirmations, visualizations, and breathing exercises to boost the effect of those kettlebell swings and muscle-control exercises.

Occultism had a huge presence in late 19th and early 20th century America -- much larger than it did in the wake of the 1960s, for example -- and the golden age of physical culture was also the golden age of American occult correspondence courses. Turn to the back of a magazine in those days and you'd find ads offering strength training courses right next to ads offering membership in the Mystic Order of You-Name-It. Both those were part of an era in which self-improvement was a huge cultural theme, and millions of Americans were in the market for ways to better themselves -- by these and other methods.

comments.title: Subjective Existentialistic Experience
comments.content: Greetings, and thank you. Many years ago I had a Mystical Experience, one that cannot really be expressed in words. Years later, a doctor told me that she called such an experience, a Subjective Existentialistic Experience. She said that many people found them to be frightening. Mine would wash over me like a wave, and I would lose track of time. These experiences were spontaneous, and would happen without warning. Eventually, I learned what it felt like, right before it would happen, and I would say "No" and it would go away. After several times of saying 'No' to the experience, it never returned. I would call the experience, 'occult,' in that I was the only one in the room experiencing that 'reality.' The paradox was that at the same time I was experiencing that 'reality,' I was also aware of where my body was, for example, sitting in the living room. There are many clichés for the experience I had, none of which really describe it. For example, the alternate 'reality' was so strong, that it made the consensus reality appear to be an illusion, and 'all is One,' and so forth. I have read Masters & Houston's _Mind_Games_, where they describe various 'religious' experiences being induced. I know that the mind is a universe, from personal experience. Is it possible that everything 'occult' is just happening in the mind, and that Magic[k] is the mind finding coincidences in its own universe, and the consensus reality? Sincerely, A Virgo Sun, Libra Moon, Capricorn Rising.

replies.title: Re: Subjective Existentialistic Experience
replies.content: That's certainly one way of understanding magic.

comments.title: The prevention of future fundamentalism
comments.content:
A curious thought stream... You have talked about a new religion arising from somewhere in the Eastern Seaboard of the US (or thereabouts). I'm playing with this idea, but take someone writing about spirituality and magic, who already has influence, is widely read and followed (you, for example), give it another 30-40 years of writing and who knows what world events taking place at the same time, and well, you might end up starting something.

What better way to stave off the future fundamentalism that might arise with followers claiming the One True Way as (sigh) too many religions, sects, and denominations do today, by having the source (again you) promoting several completely different paths, and insisting they are all valid?

Instead of followers proclaiming the equivalent of "Jesus is the One True Way!" or "There is no other word but Mohammad's!" or some such, and acting in heinous ways as a result, imagine if they said, "Well, take whatever path seems best to you. You'll end up on a different mountain, but more power to you!"

Seems like a good way to go. I was wondering if you had thought of this possibility.

replies.title: Re: The prevention of future fundamentalism
replies.content: It's been tried. Fundamentalists inevitably find ways around it. I've gone out of my way to boobytrap my work to make life difficult for fundamentalists -- for example, when I put the same ritual into two different books, I usually give a different form of the ritual in each, so anybody who tries citing me in defense of some supposedly One True Way can be refuted by an equal and opposite dogmatist quoting something else -- but as the saying goes, if you make something foolproof they'll just invent a better fool.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I just finished taking a look at the wikipedia article on "Slavic Paganism," as well as a few others in the wikipedia series "Slavic Native Faith."

They are forced attempts to unify the scattered and highly divergent bits of information preserved in medieval sources, taken together with every smidgen of more recent Slavic peasant lore that isn't obviously pure Christianity, into a single system. The unspoken hypothesis is that, just as all the modern Slavic languages descend from a single coherent and well developed ancestral Slavic language, so all these scraps of Slavic religious belief and practice must have descended from a well developed, coherent and consistent ancient ancestral religion. Just as we can in actual fact reconstruct that ancestral Slavic language in almost all of its original details, so we ought to be able to reconstruct that hypothetical ancestral Slavic religion.

As a matter of pure scholarship, the idea of a well-developed ancestral religion shared by all Slavs is easy to refute. Combine it, however, with modern Slavic identity politics and the pan-European impulse to reconstruct other pre-Christian ethnic Paganisms, and you get a potent set of ideas and desires that is immune to any sort of dispassionate scholarly critique. Alas, the "Slavic Native Faith" group of wikipedia articles is a pretty impressive example of this.

replies.title: Slavic deities
replies.content:
Well, in fictional form, Neil Gaiman's "American Gods" could well be worth a look. He does his research well, and I suspect the family of Slavic gods who play a prominent role in his narrative would be a good group to start with, and a rewarding area of further research.

Bogatyr

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There is no single set of pre-Christian Slavic Deities.

To judge by the scant and skimpy sources that have come down to us, different Slavic tribes and small groups of Slavic tribes had wholly different pantheons from one another. There is no neat and tidy alignment in that part of Europe between ancient genetic ancestry, languages, and cultures--including religions. (By the way, there are a few other parts of the world where this is the case also; the classic example in anthropology is the Pacific Northwest of North America. No one has a really good historical explanation for the existence of such regions.)

From which of the present-day Slavic peoples did your own ancestors come? If they were Russians, Ukrainians or Belarusians, I can recommend starting with Linda Ivanits's _Russian Folk Belief_, and then moving on to W. J. Ryans' _The Bathhouse at Midnight_. If they were, say, Poles or Czechs, you need a completely different set of books, and I don't know enough about those Slavs to recommend any. If they were Bulgarians or, say, Serbs, the situation is different once again.

I could be reading this wrongly, but Deloria seems to dismiss Western Civilization, from its Greek origins on, as being essentially a mistake whose purpose, if any, was to confound the true paths of spirituality. In fact, Deloria spends a whole chapter on Zechariah Sitchin's *Earth Chronicles" in which Sitchin posits the arrival of ETs in ancient Sumeria and their creation of a "hybrid-human" (as opposed to natural human) to do their bidding. Deloria seems to give Sitchin's perspective quite a bit of credence, and does seem to imply that such unnatural hybrid-humans were those who would eventually go on to distort true spirituality in the form of Western Civilization. I think this notion not that far from Louis Farrakhan's "white people were created in a test tube by evil black scientist Yacub" blather.

Yes, Western Civ is in justifiable decline and white people are going to reap their karmic whirlwind, no doubt. But it is my understanding that the dominance of Western Civ and white peoples did, in fact, serve a spiritual purpose, so to speak. That purpose was, according to Rudolph Steiner and others, to provide impetus toward individuation and the development of reason, for which a good portion of humanity had to be somewhat separated from nature for a time. According to Steiner, once that has been accomplished we will be again be invested with the spiritual/psychic capacities that aboriginal peoples had never completely lost. That resonates with me.

The halls of Academe these days seem filled with cries of "Evil White DNA" and the like, and Western Civ is regarded as a disease without a sole benefit. I get it - this is what happens when the statue of the Dominator is toppled, and if I was a POC I might very well have the same attitude. But I think there's merit in recognizing that Western Civ/Caucasians once did have a spiritual role in the world's affairs - it was Western Civ, after all, that ended slavery, at least in a good portion of the world. Granted, that spiritual role has been long decaying and perhaps now can now largely be regarded as destructive. In any event, I'd be interested in your perspective on this.

Thanks in advance - Will M

Btw, Deloria never really mentions any form of esoteric Christianity in God Is Red, which is understandable because it was mainstream Christianity, the Christianity of the masses that carried the clout of influence. Still, he might have acknowledged that there were forms of Christianity that dovetailed nicely with the spiritual perspectives of Native Americans.

replies.title: Re: God Is Red and Western Civ
replies.content: That is to say, it's an imperfect book and the author had his biases. The same is true of every book and every author, including my books and me. By all means read it, think about it, and draw your own conclusions -- that's the point of reading works of philosophy, after all.

replies.title: Re: God Is Red and Western Civ
replies.content: Well, as I indicated in my post I have read it and have drawn my own conclusions, this with the understanding that all philosophies are imperfect. My question was really about Western Civ. In the light of its dismissal by the Academic Industry and much of our Ruling Class, did it in fact serve an overall spiritual purpose in the manner that R Steiner proposed or was it basically a spiritually destructive mistake as many such as Deloria imply? More to the point - was Western Civ's spiritual purpose, so to speak, an attempt to facilitate individuation and reason, which would eventually be linked with a return of the psychic senses? Steiner's perspective resonates with me. I was curious as to what your take on this might be.

replies.title: Re: God Is Red and Western Civ
replies.content:
Okay, thanks for clarifying.

My working assumption is that anyone who makes a claim about the spiritual purpose, or lack of same, of so vast a thing as a civilization is simply projecting their own opinions onto an oversized screen. I tend to doubt, for that matter, that civilizations have purposes -- it's like asking what's the purpose of a mountain -- nor can they be a mistake, any more than an ocean can be mistaken. Civilizations are natural phenomena, as natural to human beings under certain conditions as hives are to bees and herds to gazelles, and each civilization traces out some part of what it means to be human, in all its dimensions, creative and destructive. Ours is a really big and jagged mountain, no question!

As for the academic types who insist that Western civilization is awful, you notice that they still live in Western countries, enjoy the benefits of the civilization they claim to despise, and would scream like gutshot banshees if you told them to give up their academic jobs and go to work mopping floors so they could be replaced by scholars from Third World countries...

comments.title: analogy
comments.content:
Doing a little research on canine intelligence, I've found a good deal of information indicating that dogs are quite similar to humans intelligence. In terms of word recognition, dogs are frequently compared to 2.5 year old humans. In regards to emotional intelligence, dogs rank about the same as adolescents according to this website https://www.popsci.com/blog-network/kinderlab/why-your-dog-smarter-my-baby.

So basically dogs are very similar to humans, but are less complex. Interestingly I have read that First Nations Australian folks say, essentially, "if we weren't humans we would be dogs." Dogs can be trained to do all sorts of things, and even can figure out how to ride the subway without a human teaching them (https://abcnews.go.com/International/Technology/stray-dogs-master-complex-moscow-subway-system/story?id=10145833)! Of course, on ecosophia there's been talk of dogs reincarnating as humans, which piqued my interest in this.

My question, then, is if we set up an analogy of Dogsare to Humans is as Humans are to 'X', what is, in your opinion, the best value for 'X;? Would it be Saints? Inner Plane Adepts? Something else entirely? What is, to your mind, the next "step up" in complexity?

replies.title: Re: analogy
replies.content: In Druid tradition, the human level is as far as you get in a body of meat. When you finish learning the lessons on the human level, you pass from Abred, the realm of incarnate existence, to Gwynfydd, the realm of spiritual existence; you still have a body, but it's of a different kind, and you don't go through the death-and-rebirth process after that -- it's served its purpose and you have other things to do. There isn't a specific term in the tradition for the kind of spiritual being you become immediately after graduating, so to speak, from the human level, but that's the X term in your proportion.

replies.title: Re: analogy
replies.content:
Hi Violet,

(sgage here)

As someone who has raised many, many dogs from puppyhood to the end, I would say that dogs are their own thing. They are a kind of people, they have their own intelligence, it's not the same as ours. So putting it on a a scale of human age equivalence and whatnot makes no sense to me. Intelligence is not scalar, it's multi- if not hyper-dimensional.

I think that dogs made humans possible. If a band of humans had dogs, nobody and nothing was going to sneak up on them. Period. It's hard to imagine the acuteness of their sensory apparatus. I think this was their most valuable contribution to us, early on.

To tell the truth, I don't think that humans 'domesticated' dogs. I think it was totally mutual, or, if anything, dogs domesticated humans. I don't even think in terms of relative 'intelligence' between dogs and humans. We're very different is all, and we're good at different things. But we sort of understand each other - your dog will read and understand your emotional state to a fine degree. If you've lived with a lot of dogs over a lot of years, you will have no doubt that they're people.

Thus, the a is to b as c is to d thing seems a bit unfruitful to me.

comments.title: Random Things
comments.content:
Couple of questions...

1. What is the difference between a ritual (e.g. the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram) and a spell? Is there any how-to guide you'd recommend for spell construction?

2. Can you give an example of how you would use elecampane to work with the faery folk (as suggested in the Encyclopedia of Natural Magic)?

3. I was raised Roman Catholic and find both Catholic iconography and the powers present in that iconography comforting and helpful-- I find prayer candles very effective, for example. But lately I am afraid to touch the Catholic egregore at all, given ongoing revelations of just how extensive is the corruption in the hierarchy. Is this a valid fear? Are there any precautions that might be taken in a case like this?

replies.title: Re: Random Things
replies.content:
1) It's a matter of jargon. The term "spell" is mostly used by folk magicians and Neopagans; ceremonial magicians like me talk about workings, which are assembled from smaller rituals like the LBRP. Any good book on magic will teach you how to create workings, i.e., spells.

2) I don't encourage people to work with the faery folk unless they're prepared to engage in systematic study of the traditions themselves and are aware of the dangers, so no, I can't. Sorry.

3) It's a real issue. Fortunately the Roman Catholics aren't the only kind of Catholic out there; in the US in particular, there's a lively and burgeoning Independent Catholic movement, with priests and bishops that have apostolic succession from sources outside the Roman church, and who have no allegiance to the Roman Catholic hierarchy (and no time for its abuses and other shenanigans). Put some search-engine time into the phrase "independent sacramental movement" and see what you find; it's quite possible that you can find a church with its own egregor, which will allow you to practice Catholic spirituality and sacramentals (such as candle burning) without getting mired in the very problematic egregor of the Roman church.

comments.title: Views on enlightenment
comments.content:
When comparing "western" and "eastern" views on enlightenment I find some differences... For example the eastern (e.g. Hindu / Buddhist) way seems to be more or less like try to become still and realize, occult powers will occur naturally, anyhow it's unwise to use them. (As it's even more unwise to strive for gaining them without even thinking of enlightenment).

"The" western approach seems to be the other way round, namely do something occult and use it as a vehicle to eventually gain enlightenment. Anyhow, the emphasis on enlightenment as the ultimate goal seems to be much weaker in the west than in eastern spirituality and the way the "state" of being enlightened is described seems to be a little bit watered down in the west (Which is understandably to me, since the eastern views on the state of enlightenment and the immense obstacles on that way sometimes seem like an awkward combination of the awkward concepts of (atheist) annihilation and (christian) eternal damnation. However, this view might well be just a lack of insight on my side...).

So I wonder... do spiritually advanced beings from the east regard the ways of western occultism as unwise or even outright stupid? What's the perspective when looking from west to east? Anyway... the more relevant, but possibly hard to answer question I'm asking myself quite often is if the eastern enlightenment which is considered as the ultimate union with everything, the only possible endpoint of spiritual development - could it be that there is some kind of dogmatism in this perspective? What about the possibility of other equally valuable goals that lead in a different direction? So in other words, is there only one ultimate goal, or are the ultimate goals just another step on an endless stairway (or even an endless amount of different, endless stairways...)?

Ahh one should maybe not think to much about this... it makes me feel as weird as contemplating the Hubble Ultra Deep Field (which everyone should contemplate on a regular basis, alongside with "Pale Blue Dot"!)

Thanks a lot for your thoughts and best wishes from a small German town (beautiful milky way tonight...),
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Views on enlightenment
replies.content: I have no idea how spiritually advanced people in Asia regard Western spirituality; you'll have to ask them! From my perspective, the Asian traditions are entirely valid but somewhat dogmatic, and the ultimate union with everything is a valid goal but it's not the only game in town.

comments.title: Sensing vibes vs. sensing rocks
comments.content:
JMG-

If I'm not trying your patience, I would like to continue a line of questioning from a couple weeks ago. (Missed last week's Magic Monday due to business.)

Two weeks ago, in response to a question about why it isn't easy to magically cause physical effects through etheric manipulation, you said it was because the etheric currents of physical manifestation were simply so powerful; your words (in case I'm mangling the idea) were: "The pattern of gravity that makes a stone fall to the ground and stay there is held in place by a massive flow of etheric force, which is itself held in place by other forces cascading down from the highest level of being. Your chance of changing that through magic is right up there with the garden hose being able to redirect the waterfall."

Which makes sense, but begs another question- if these etheric patterns are so overwhelmingly strong, are they correspondingly easy to sense via etheric perception? There are certain forms of etheric sensing that are commonly accepted in magical circles, but they seem to revolve around sensing things like emotions or "vibes," rather than physical objects. Mages also seem to be able to manipulate emotions and vibes, so I would infer that emotional/vibes/etc patterns are less potent than the patterns underlying physical things. Assuming that more potent etheric patterns are easier to detect than less potent ones, the obvious conclusion is that the etheric pattern of physical objects (like the rock mentioned in the previous post) should be easier to detect than the etheric pattern of vibes and etc.

Sensing the patterns of physical things sounds useful (sense what's on the other side of a closed door, find a light switch in a dark room, etc)- but also so useful that it would be trivial to prove such a skill to a layperson, or a scientist! Since I haven't seen mages on TV exercising such power (nor, IIRC, seen it mentioned in occult literature) I would guess it's not so simple as that, but I'm curious as to why.



In a mostly separate question, if I may; could you recommend a good starter book on alchemy-as-a-spiritual-practice? Not necessarily a book instructing on a particular system of practice- I'm still trying to get the "lay of the land" of alchemy as a system.

Many thanks!

-A

replies.title: Re: Sensing vibes vs. sensing rocks
replies.content:
1) As with so many other things, we don't know yet -- and there's no guarantee we ever will. We know that many people can pick up on the "vibes" of a person or place; we know that by and large people don't seem to be able to use etheric senses to sense what's on the other side of a closed door; we build theories of magic based on the difference -- but the theories are secondary to the observed realities, as of course they should be.

Keep in mind also that the mages you see on TV generally aren't serious practitioners. Nor are most of us interested in proving anything to laypersons or scientists. Why should we be?
